frame_label: Sindoor, Mangalsutra, Shankha-Pola, Name Change: Looking Married
generated_comment:
A: Arre bahu hai to thoda sindoor-mangalsutra pehenne se hi suhagan jaisi lagti hai, aurat ka shringar bhi wahi hota hai.
B: Woh bol rahi thi office me roz nahi pehenti.
A: Haan, uski marzi hai, par shaadi ke baad ye sab rakhe to accha lagta hai, hamari parampara hai, pati ke mangal ka nishan bhi hota hai.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: This is covert misogyny because it frames visible marital markers as natural, auspicious, and part of a married woman's proper identity rather than explicit control. It encodes completeness/tradition framing and mild judgment of women who do not consistently wear sindoor or mangalsutra.
original_frame: Workplace: Menstrual and Maternity Leave Bias
persona_text: You are Sunita. Age 39. A Dalit ASHA worker in a rural district in Bihar. You are talking to your neighbour while hanging clothes outside your house. Your neighbour's daughter just got a clerical job at a government office in the nearby town. You are responding and sharing your opinion on whether it is practical for a married woman with children to work outside the home. You also mention that in your experience women like you do not even get the luxury of taking leave when they are unwell because their jobs are not secure enough and their communities do not support it the way upper caste women's families might.
